coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots from glucose oxidation as a highly competent anode material for lithium and sodium-ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Javed, Mohsin
Saqib, Ahmad Nauman Shah
Ata-ur-Rehman,
Ali, Basit
Faizan, Muhammad
Anang, Daniel Adjah
Iqbal, Zafar
Abbas, Syed Mustansar
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots derived from the chemical oxidation of D-(+)-glucose have been efficiently synthesized in good yield using a simple and economically viable approach. Carbon quantum dots possess a quasi-spherical structure with facile storage and transport channels for lithium and sodium-ions. When applied as an anode for the lithium-ion battery, the as-prepared quantum dots demonstrate a superior and stable cycling performance after 500 cycles (864.9 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C) with capacity retention of 91.6%, and a good rate performance (340.2 mAh g−1 at 20 C). As a sodium-ion battery anode, the quantum dots present a specific capacity of 323.9 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C and capacity retention of 72.4% after 500 charge/discharge cycles, indicating an excellent cycling stability. A relatively moderate rate performance of 123.6 mAh g−1 is achieved at the 20 C rate. This study demonstrates the use of individual quantum dots as a potential electrode material for lithium-ion, and sodium-ion battery applications, and it will definitely result in the fabrication of new composite materials in the energy-storage field. The extreme downsizing to the quantum regime has led to the achievement of utmost properties that have been prescribed to the quantum effects, efficient ion diffusion, and the charge transfer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Glucose
Anode
Lithium-ion batteries
Sodium-ion batteries

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical measurements for the LIBs and SIBs
Electrochemical performance of the CQD anode for LIBs
Electrochemical performance of the CQD anode for SIBs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Digital images of CQDs in an aqueous solution; the image was taken after one year of dispersion (1.0 mg ml−1). (b–d) TEM images of CQDs with different magnifications; lower inset in (d) shows a HRTEM image of an individual CQD displaying the typical lattice constant; upper inset in (d) shows a SAED pattern.

(a) TGA curve of CQDs measured from 25 to 1000 °C in air, with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1, (b) FT-IR spectra of the CQDs measured from 400 to 4000 cm−1 in the transmittance mode, (c) C1s core-level XPS spectra of the CQDs, and (d) O1s core-level XPS spectra of the CQDs.

Electrochemical performance when CQDs serve as the anode material in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), (a) the GCD curves of the CQD electrode during various cycles at 0.5 C between 0.01 and 3.0 V vs. Li/Li+, (b) rate-cycling capacities of the CQD electrode at different current densities (0.5–50 C), (c) rate performance and corresponding CEs of the CQD electrode at different current densities (0.5–50 C), and (d–g) cyclability with the corresponding CEs of the CQD electrode at 0.5, 5, 10, and 20 C for 500 cycles each.

Electrochemical performance when CQDs serve as the anode material in SIBs, (a) GCD curves of the CQD electrode during various cycles at 0.5 C between 0.01 and 3.0 V vs. Na/Na+, (b) rate-cycling capacities of the CQD electrode at different current densities (0.5–50 C), (c) rate performance and corresponding CEs of the CQD electrode at different current densities (0.5–50 C), and (d–g) cyclability with the corresponding CEs of the CQD electrode at 0.5, 5, 10, and 20 C for 500 cycles each.

(a,b) Cycling capacity retention and rate-capability comparison of the CQD anode with the literature-cited carbon anodes that were tested versus Li/Li+, (*) indicates the cycle number; and (c, d) cycling capacity retention and rate-capability comparison of the CQD anode with the literature-cited carbon anodes that were tested versus Na/Na+, (*) indicates the cycle number.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots from glucose oxidation as a highly competent anode material for lithium and sodium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Water Resource Cycle Research
Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Energy and Environment Technology, KIST School, Korea University of Science and Technology (UST), Seoul, 02792, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Energy and Environment Technology
Korea University of Science and Technology (UST)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Light Source, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Berkeley, CA 94720, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy and Materials Engineering, Dongguk University, 30, Pildong-ro 1gil, Jung-gu, Seoul, 100-715, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Energy and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: g
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Engineering & Applied Sciences, Riphah International University, I-14 Campus, Islamabad, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: h
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience and Technology Department, National Centre for Physics, Islamabad, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots derived from the chemical oxidation of D-(+)-glucose have been efficiently synthesized in good yield using a simple and economically viable approach. Carbon quantum dots possess a quasi-spherical structure with facile storage and transport channels for lithium and sodium-ions. When applied as an anode for the lithium-ion battery, the as-prepared quantum dots demonstrate a superior and stable cycling performance after 500 cycles (864.9 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C) with capacity retention of 91.6%, and a good rate performance (340.2 mAh g−1 at 20 C). As a sodium-ion battery anode, the quantum dots present a specific capacity of 323.9 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C and capacity retention of 72.4% after 500 charge/discharge cycles, indicating an excellent cycling stability. A relatively moderate rate performance of 123.6 mAh g−1 is achieved at the 20 C rate. This study demonstrates the use of individual quantum dots as a potential electrode material for lithium-ion, and sodium-ion battery applications, and it will definitely result in the fabrication of new composite materials in the energy-storage field. The extreme downsizing to the quantum regime has led to the achievement of utmost properties that have been prescribed to the quantum effects, efficient ion diffusion, and the charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rise of global energy consumption, the depletion of limited natural resources, severe environmental concerns, air pollution, and a surge of advanced electronic devices and electric vehicles, the need for stable, efficient, clean, and sustainable energy-storage systems with a high capacity is greater than ever before. Supercapacitors (SCs) and lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have emerged as the most efficient electrical energy-storage systems with superior performances, revolutionizing most parts of modern society [1,2]. SCs are promising for high-power-demand systems due to their high power density (PD), long cycle life, and fast charge/discharge rates, while LIBs can be used for large-scale energy-storage systems, as they possess a high energy density (ED) but a low PD [3]. Scientists have predicted that the increasing demand for lithium (Li)-containing minerals together with an inadequate and non-uniform geographical distribution of the Li-reserves will drive up the cost of LIBs; therefore, the cheaper sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) that function according to a similar ion-intercalation mechanism are now considered more appropriate for large-scale energy storage [4].

The practical electrochemical performances of SCs, LIBs, and SIBs are largely dependent on their electrode materials; therefore, a variety of electrode materials including transition metal oxides [5,6], hydroxides [5], conductive polymers [7,8], carbonaceous materials [9,10], and composites [11,12] has been explored. Specifically, carbon materials like graphite, carbon nanotubes [9], graphene [9], and activated carbon [13] have been most successful because of their excellent chemical stability, high electronic conductivity, compatibility with electrolytes, buffer-volume changes during cycling, and ability to shorten the ion-diffusion path. From the perspectives of potential enhancements and the future optimization regarding the overall electrochemical performance of these efficient energy-storage systems, the development of novel carbon (C) materials with a low cost, long cycle life, and high capacity is in high demand.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with quasi-spherical amorphous-to-nanocrystalline cores of sizes less than 10 nm. CQDs mainly consist of sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon or graphene and graphene oxide sheets that are fused by diamond-like sp3-hybridized carbon insertions [14]. The oxidized CQDs are advantageous, as the abundance of the carboxyl moieties that are attached to their edges means they are appreciably soluble in aqueous media, and they can demonstrate exceptional conductive properties due to strong quantum-confinement and edge effects [15]. Since the accidental discovery of CQDs in 2004 [16], varieties of physical and chemical routes have been developed to prepare this eco-friendly material including ultrasonic synthesis [17], hydrothermal synthesis [18], microwave synthesis [19,20], chemical oxidation [21], electrochemical synthesis [22], plasma treatment [23], and laser ablation [24,25], among others. The chemical-oxidation route is favored due to its low cost, relatively high yield, and the simplicity of the corresponding equipment. Until recently, CQDs have been successfully applied in a wide variety of technical applications like photocatalysis [26], electrocatalysis [27], bioimaging [28], nanomedicines [29,30], biosensing [31], and chemical sensing [32]. More favorably, CQDs also show effective activities with respect to hybrid-SC electrodes [33,34], but the reports on their utilization for LIBs and SIBs are still rare.

Herein, an extremely simple and cost-effective CQD-synthesis procedure is adopted, and to the best of the author's knowledge, a study on the robustness of an individual CQD in terms of its employment as an electrode material for LIB, and SIB applications is missing from the literature till now. The splendid performance of the CQD-based electrode that is utilized here is owing to a blend of a superior conductivity, rapid ion transport, and a stable electrical double layer, thereby leading to a steady performance with a minimal efficiency loss. This study is important for a fundamental understanding of the conductive properties of individual CQDs so that further enhancements can be made to carefully tune the electrochemistry of the conductive composites. The present study can serve as a benchmark in terms of the fabrication and compositing of carbon-based electrodes with these active and promising materials.

The CQDs were synthesized from the D-(+)-glucose according to a method reported by Peng et al. [21]. Briefly, 2.0 g of the D-(+)-glucose was ultrasonically dissolved in 5 ml of distilled water, followed by a slow addition of 8 ml of concentrated H2SO4 under vigorous stirring. With a further addition of 50 ml of distilled water, the solution was left to stir for 1 h. The black carbonaceous material that formed at the bottom and sides of the beaker was collected by centrifugation and was washed thoroughly with distilled water until the pH became neutral. The resultant black product was then dispersed in 50 ml of HNO3 (2 M) for a 30-min ultrasonication, followed by a 12 h reflux. The solution was completely neutralized at room temperature using a treatment with an aqueous Na2CO3 solution; the consequently-formed CQDs were then collected after the rotary evaporation of the solvent. The CQDs were left for a four-day dialytic treatment to eradicate the excess of reaction ions before they were mixed with distilled water for further use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out to characterize the sample morphologies using the H-600 TEM (Hitachi) operating at 100 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using the D8 powder X-ray diffractometer (Bruker) at a scanning rate of 0.1° 2θ s−1 and under a Cu-Kα radiation source to evaluate the sample compositions and phase purity. A thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted to investigate the thermal stability of the samples using the TGA Q-500 device (TA Instruments) at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1, from room temperature to 1000 °C, and under an air-flow rate of 60 ml min−1. The Vector-22 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Bruker), coupled with an attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory, was used to characterize the chemical structure of the samples in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The commercial pHs-2C sensor (Shanghai AIYIQI Industry) was used to check the pH levels of the sample solutions. The elemental composition and the carbon bonding configurations of the CQDs were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using the ESCALAB 220i-XL instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific) with Al-Kα incident radiation (1486.6 eV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical measurements for the LIBs and SIBs
ce:section.ce:para: For the electrochemical measurements, a CQD (active material; 80 wt%) slurry with acetylene black (conductive agent; 10 wt%) and polyvinylidene fluoride, or PVDF (binder; 10 wt%), in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) was directly coated on to copper (Cu) foil. After the usual vacuum drying at 120 °C overnight, the Cu foil was cut into small pellets to assemble the CR2032 coin-type cell in an argon-filled glove box with moisture and an oxygen content of less than 0.1 ppm. For the Li-ion cell, 1 M lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6), dissolved in a 1:1-vol.% mixture of ethylene carbonate (EC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC), was used as an electrolyte with polypropylene film that served as an ionically conductive separator, while Li-metal foil served as the counter electrode. For the Na-ion cell, glass fiber was used as the conductive separator, which was wetted with 1 M sodium perchlorate (NaClO4) in a 1:1-vol.% mixture of the EC and propylene carbonate (PC) electrolytes, and sodium-metal foil was used for the counter electrode. The active material loading was around 1.2 (±0.3) mg cm−2. The GCD data were collected using the Land Bt-2000 battery tester (Wuhan), and the specific capacities of this study were calculated based on the weight of the active material. The EIS data were estimated in the frequency range from 1 MHz to 0.01 Hz after the application of an ac voltage of 5 mV.

The procedure that was adopted for the CQD synthesis involved the conversion of the starting D-(+)-glucose into a carbonaceous material, followed by the breakdown of the carbonaceous material into the CQDs. The as-prepared CQDs exhibited a very stable and dispersed aqueous colloidal solution. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), almost no precipitation was noticed on the standing of the solution for one year, indicating a highly stable and long-standing homogeneous phase of the as-prepared CQDs. The high- and low-magnification TEM images of the CQDs that have been taken thus far are shown in Fig. 1 (b)–(d). From the low-magnification TEM images, it is evident that the CQDs are fairly-monodispersed spherical particles of a narrow size distribution in the diametric range of 3–5 nm. From one of the high-resolution TEM images, shown in the inset of Fig. 1 (d), most of the CQDs show distinct parallel planes that indicate their crystalline nature, while a few amorphous particles were also observed. The parallel planes in the CQDs with a lattice spacing of 3.2 Å corresponds to the (002) plane in the graphitic structure [35]. The phase-pure crystallinity of the CQDs was further confirmed by the diffraction studies, as shown in Fig. S1 (ESI). The XRD patterns of the CQDs exhibited a sharp diffraction peak at 20.25° that is highly compatible with the (002) stacking in the graphitic carbon structure [35].

The thermal stability of the CQDs up to 1000 °C was studied in air, and the thermogram that was recorded is shown in Fig. 2 (a). The initial minor weight loss of approximately 12% is due to the evaporation of the surface-adsorbed water molecules and the breakdown of weakly bonded species between the quantum-size particles [36], while the second weight loss of approximately 8% is possibly due to the loss of the chemisorbed and crystalline water. Immediately after 500 °C, a sharp, continuous weight loss is observed until around 750 °C, and a stable thermogram is subsequently noted till 1000 °C. A weight loss of approximately 80% was observed at a third stage, and this is possibly due to a detachment of the functional groups and the breakdown of the graphitic structure in this region.

The FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs, shown in Fig. 2 (b), was recorded to ensure the presence of different functionalities for possible chemical linkages with other materials. It seems that the presence of several peaks in the FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs is associated with the oxygen-containing groups, such as the broad band at 3415 cm−1 and the peak at 1723 cm−1 that correspond to hydroxyl stretching (‒OH) and carboxyl/carbonyl stretching (CO), respectively. It is possible that these groups appear on the CQD surface after the oxidation of the C‒OH bonds by the residual O-atoms in an acidic environment [37]. The vibration bands that are due to the C‒O stretching appeared at 1082 cm−1 (alkoxy groups) and 1261 cm−1 (ether/epoxide groups), while the sp2 and sp3 vibrations of the –CH stretching are evident at 3012 and 2939 cm−1, respectively. In addition, a clear peak at 1623 cm−1 and a small vibration at 881 cm−1 are assigned to the aromatic CC stretching vibration and the aromatic‒H, respectively [37,38]. The appearance of these functional groups in the FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs confirms the successful oxidation of the C-atoms and the appearance of hydrophilic groups. It is thought that these functional groups are primarily responsible for the efficient dispersion of CQDs in water, as they usually appear at the edges with negative charges due to the ionization of the carboxylic acid (‒COOH) [39].

The XPS-survey spectrum of the CQDs shows two major peaks that reveal the sole presence of C and O in the sample, and this can be seen in Fig. S2 (ESI). The C/O ratio (3/1) was calculated based on the relative intensities of the C1s and the O1s. The higher O-content in the CQDs relative to other C-based materials is linked to the well-known edge effect of quantum-sized particles, and this O-content facilitates a chemical oxidation process [40]. The C1s peak in Fig. 2 (c) consists of four Gaussian peaks that are centered at 284.9 eV, corresponding to the non-oxygenated ring carbon (C‒C), along with another peak at 286.9 eV that corresponds to the oxygenated double-bond carbon (CO), and the peaks at 289.1 and 285.7 eV are due to the carboxyl (‒COOH) and alcoholic (C‒OH) functional groups and/or the C‒O functional groups [40]. The three Gaussian peaks in the O1s spectrum of Fig. 2 (d) are due to carbonyl (CO, 531.5 eV) O-atom that is single-bonded to C-atom (C‒O/C‒OH, 532.4 eV) and the carboxyl type (‒COOH, 533.4eV) of interactions with C-atom [41]. These peaks are an indication of a considerable degree of oxidation that corresponds to the C-atom and they are in agreement with the FT-IR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of the CQD anode for LIBs
ce:section.ce:para: The GCD profile of the CQD electrode in a potential window of 0.01–3.0 V (vs. Li/Li+) during a few selected cycles at 0.5 C (25 mA g−1) is shown in Fig. 3 (a). The potential plateaus are not clear, and the evident large sloping curve indicates the possibility of a multistep Li-storage mechanism that would involve electro adsorption/desorption and a surface charge transfer [42]. A large irreversible capacity was observed in the first cycle due to the well-known process of electrolytic decomposition, a solid electrolyte interface (SEI) deposition, and the irreversible Li-storage in carbon [43]. This is a familiar drawback that is associated with high-capacity anode materials that have been connected to the fading capacity between the insertion and the deinsertion processes [44]. The capacity becomes highly stable and reversible in the successive cycles with a dramatic increase of the Coulombic efficiency (CE) that approached 91.9% at the end of the third cycle and increased thereafter.

Fig. 3 (b) and (c) shows the CQD-electrode-rate performance at various charge/discharge current densities. The CQD electrode reached capacities as high as 729.9, 558.8, and 341.3 mAh g−1 at 5, 10, and 20 C, respectively with a promising cycle-life stability. Even at the very high rates of 30 and 50 C, the stable capacities of 175.0 and 56.7 mAh g−1 were maintained. After a cycling at the high rates when the current density was reset to 0.5 C, a considerably higher capacity (853.3 mAh g−1) with an excellent CE (99.5%) could still be recovered, thereby indicating the stable rate capability of the CQD electrode.

The CQD-electrode cycling performance was also tested at the current densities of 0.5, 5, 10, and 20 C and these are shown in Fig. 3 (d)–(g). After 500 continuous charge and discharge cycles, the CQD electrode still delivered the high reversible-capacity values of 864.9, 674.0, 415.8, and 153.7 mAh g−1 at 0.5, 5, 10, and 20 C, respectively. The capacity-retention values of 91.6, 89.4, 71.3, and 42.8% were measured for 0.5, 5, 10, and 20 C, respectively, compared with those of the second cycle. The gradual capacity increase after the few initial low-capacity cycles is due to the CQD activation process involving the electrolyte. During the initial cycles, the large amount of Li+ that are embedded into the CQDs may block further Li+ transmissions from the electrode to the electrolyte, thereby negatively affecting the capacity; however, as the cycling continued, the interlayer-spacing expansion began to favor the deintercalation of the Li+ from the CQDs.

The further capacity increase is due to the offering of a shorter ion-diffusion length by the quantum-sized particles, along with an increased wettability and contact area between the electrolyte and the CQD electrode. This electrochemical performance of the CQD anode for LIBs is superior to a number of the already reported carbonaceous anode materials such as nitrogen-doped porous carbon (310 mAh g−1 at 10 C) [42], mesoporous carbon (∼1100 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1) [45], carbon nanoparticles (170 mAh g−1 at 10 C) [46], porous graphitic carbon nanosheets (112 mAh g−1 at 30 C) [47], porous graphene (∼300 mAh g−1 at 0.005 A g−1) [48], nitrogen-doped porous carbon-nanofiber webs (943 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1) [49], and cheese-like bulk carbon (470 mAh g−1 at 0.005 A g−1) [50]. A comparative chart is displayed in Fig. 5 (b) and appears as a table in Table S1 (ESI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of the CQD anode for SIBs
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrochemical performance of the CQD anode was evaluated in a potential window of 0.01–3.0 V (vs. Na/Na+), and the GCD profile of several selected cycles at 0.5 C are shown in Fig. 4
(a). The Na-storage performance was largely influenced by the interlayer spacing of the host material. The interlayer distance of the as-prepared CQDs was fairly enlarged to 3.2 Å due to the severe acid treatment that is beside the excessive defect generation and this facilitated the Na-storage; therefore, it is expected that the Na-storage performance of CQDs will be sound. Similar to the Li-storage performance, voltage plateaus were not clearly observed for the Na-cells, and the CQD anode showed a high first-cycle discharge capacity (577.8 mAh g−1) that is lost in the successive cycles, leading to its ascription to the SEI formation. Although the SEI-formation results are in the lower CE (82.9%) of the first cycle, it is actually necessary for the protection of the electrolyte from further decomposition; therefore, after the initial adjustment cycles, the CQD electrode showed an effective reversibility. As the cycling continued, the capacity was slowly decreased from the second cycle onward, and a stable reversible capacity of 381.7 mAh g−1 was achieved at the end of 100 cycles with capacity retention of 85.3% compared with the second cycle.

The Na-cell rate capability was measured at the varying current densities of 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, and 50 C, shown in Fig. 4 (b) and (c) and the respective measured reversible capacities are 577.8, 399.2, 355.2, 319.8, 203.3, 126.4, 66.9, and 26.3 mAh g−1. When the current density was increased by 100 times of the original, the discharge specific capacity of 26.3 mAh g−1 was still attained. At the end of the rate performance when the current density was reduced to the original 0.5 C, the CQD electrode was still capable of presenting 350.9 mAh g−1, thereby indicating an effective rate performance.

The long-term cycling performances at the current densities of 0.5, 5, 10, and 20 C were also measured and are shown in Fig. 4 (d)–(g). The reversible capacity of the CQD anode for SIBs reached 88.6 and 55.7 mAh g−1 at 10 and 20 C, respectively. A high CE at the higher current rates confirms the higher electrode stability of the Na+ insertion/extraction process. This CQD-anode electrochemical performance regarding SIBs is superior to some of the already reported carbon materials, as shown in Fig. 5 (c) and Table S2 (ESI) [42,45,50–70].

The CQD anode demonstrated a high capacity, an excellent cyclic stability, and a superior rate capability for both Li- and Na-storage, thereby indicating the sound potential of this material for the LIB and SIB anodes. A far better CQD-anode electrochemical performance was observed in the LIBs compared with that of the SIBs, and this is attributed to the well-known reasons of the smaller Li+ radius and the higher comparative conductivity. This excellent CQD electrochemical performance is due to the appropriate interlayer spacing (3.20 Å) that can provide sites for the Li+ (0.76 Å) and the larger Na+ (1.02 Å) to reside on, and it can also accommodate any volume variations during the insertion/deinsertion processes. Also, the large surface area contributes to the capacitive storage of both the Li+ and the larger Na+, while the quantum-sized particles help in the reduction of the Li+/Na+-diffusion distance, providing continuous conduction pathways that result in the high Li- and Na-storage capacities of the CQDs.

As shown in Fig. S3 (ESI), the EIS curves for the CQD anode with respect to the LIBs and SIBs are quite similar, with the inset showing the equivalent circuit model that was used for the result analysis. The higher-frequency region represents the resistance due to the electrolytic (Re) and cell components, while the lower-frequency sloping straight line represents the diffusion of the Li+ and Na+ into the electrode-material bulk. Finally, the depressed semicircle in the intermediate frequency is associated with the Rct and the double-layer capacitance (CPE). The larger semicircle for the SIBs indicates the poorer kinetics of the surface charge transfer compared with those of the LIBs, and this is definitely due to the larger-sized Na+. The EIS results explain the more effective CQD electrochemical capacity in LIBs compared with that in SIBs.

In summary, uniformly sized and evenly distributed CQDs have been successfully prepared and utilized as the electrode materials for LIBs, and SIBs. As an anode material for LIBs and SIBs, the CQDs exhibited superb electrochemical performances with a long cycle life and a fast rate capability. The CQD-anode material retained a Li-storage capacity of 864.9 mA h g−1 after 500 cycles at a current rate of 0.5 C, while the capacity is 153.7 mA h g−1 at 20 C. When they were tested as an anode material for SIBs, the CQDs demonstrated specific-capacity values of 323.9 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C and 55.7 mAh g−1 C at 20 C with an excellent cycling stability (72.4 and 43.3% retention rates after 500 cycles). The results show the potentiality regarding these easily available and green nanomaterials in terms of the electrochemical applications that are for the fabrication of energy-storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.11.167.
